extract_meta.description: Session B findings with no catalogue question mapped — status=pending, catalogue_question_id=NULL
extract_meta.note: These findings are generic in pattern but have not yet been matched to a specific catalogue question. They require review: (a) identify which catalogue question(s) apply, (b) add to wa_finding_catalogue_links with status=suggested, or (c) if no question exists, flag as catalogue gap candidate.

findings.finding: Groups 4652-001 and 4654-001 (H2258A cha.vol and H2258B cha.vo.lah, pledge): assigned Moral/Conscience but the inner-being filter is borderline — pledge-handling is primarily an external economic act whose moral significance is diagnostic of inner character rather than a direct inner-being engagement. Session B should examine whether the verse evidence sustains the Moral/Conscience assignment or whether these groups are better treated as externalised moral diagnostics.

Group 4651-001 (H2259 cho.vel, pilot): assigned Spiritual/God-ward but the boundary with Volitional/Will is uncertain. The helmsman figure calling the crew to prayer in extremity may name an authority/directional dimension rather than a personal God-ward orientation. Session B should examine the verse evidence to determine whether the inner-being engagement is volitional direction, spiritual appeal, or some combination.

The bondage registry shows a consistent inner-being polarity: enslavement to disordered desires/sin/fear on one side, willing surrender to God/Christ on the other. Session B should examine this polarity as the governing analytical structure — bondage as the inner-being term for what masters the person.

Registry 18 (brokenness) is the most dimensionally uniform registry in C05: all 6 groups converge on Affective/Emotional. The she.ver and related vocabulary names breaking, wounding, and shattering as inner-being conditions. This sub-theme — the inner person as something that can be broken and must be healed — is a coherent and distinctive contribution within the C05 cluster that Session B should treat as a unit.

The calling registry (Reg 19) has 13 of 24 groups (54%) assigned Divine-Human Correspondence — the highest concentration of any single registry in the programme to date. The calling vocabulary is structurally a divine-human act: God calls, names, and constitutes; the human receives, responds, and is formed. Session B should examine how the human inner person is changed by the divine call, and what distinguishes the groups where HUMAN volition or emotion is the primary inner-being phenomenon from those where the divine calling act is the governing feature.

Group 727-001 (krazō — 'Abba! Father!', Rom 8:15; Gal 4:6) is the programme's clearest example of the Spirit acting from within the human inner being: the Spirit's cry is simultaneously divine and human. This is the most acute instance of Divine-Human Correspondence encountered so far — not the Spirit acting on the human from outside, but the Spirit expressing itself through and as the human inner person's deepest cry. Session B should examine whether this represents a distinct sub-type of Divine-Human Correspondence (indwelling rather than external correspondence) that may warrant distinction in the vocabulary.

The dokimos root family (dokimazō / dokimos / dokimion / dokimē) encodes a coherent inner-being formation arc across four groups: dokimion (testing instrument = Transformation) → dokimazō in testing sense (character disclosed = Moral Character) → dokimē (proved character = Moral Character) → dokimos (the proved person = Moral Character); with dokimazō in its discernment sense (= Cognition) showing the same root serving both formation and ongoing moral discernment. Session B should examine whether this root family constitutes a unique formation arc in NT vocabulary — from testing instrument, through proved character, to the characterised person — and how it maps onto the programme's understanding of moral formation under trial.

Group 733-001 (ni.chum — compassionate comfort, Reg 23) is anchored by Hos 11:8: "my heart recoils within me; my compassion grows warm and tender." This verse names the divine inner life with visceral specificity — the heart recoiling, compassion warming. Session B for Reg 23 should examine whether this visceral inner-movement language for God represents a unique category within the compassion vocabulary: divine inner-being experience described through embodied sensation. The question for Session B is whether the compassion vocabulary of Reg 23 contains a sub-pattern of "visceral inner movement" terms (splanchnizō, ni.chum, ra.cha.mim) that share a somatic/embodied description of the inner compassion movement, and whether this sub-pattern spans the divine-human boundary (both God and humans described as "moved within" by compassion).

H4941H mish.pat appears in both Reg 24 (condemnation) and Reg 98 (justice) — three groups each. Session B must read both registries' mish.pat groups together to form a complete inner-being profile of this term.

Group 3090-001 (hēdonē — pleasure as the competing inner orientation, desires that war within) occupies a significant counter-position to the positive delight vocabulary. Pleasure as the adversary of the will toward God is a distinctive NT inner-being concept. Session B should examine the hēdonē / delight tension as a structural feature of the inner life.

Reg 50 shows disobedience operating across three distinct inner-being dimensions in only 4 groups: Character/Disposition (defining orientation), Spiritual/God-ward (self-exclusion from divine rest), and Moral/Conscience (covenantal consequence). Session B should examine whether disobedience is primarily a dispositional, relational, or moral-act category in Scripture.

Group 851-004 ('the impossibility of inner self-knowledge — the heart's moral corruption exceeds human capacity to understand') is a theologically significant statement about the opacity of the heart to itself. Session B should treat this as a key inner-being anthropological claim and examine its relationship to groups in other registries (e.g. conscience, self-knowledge, moral awareness).

Group 858-003 (ra.ah — seeing triggering inner response) captures a structural principle: the visual/perceptual act as a trigger mechanism for inner emotional and volitional activation. The group names a spectrum from desire to anger. Session B should examine whether ra.ah's role as inner-state activator is a consistent structural function across the Scripture corpus — specifically whether sight as inner trigger constitutes a distinct inner-being mechanism worth tracking in the programme vocabulary. The Gen 3:6 anchor (the Fall) and Job 32:5 (Elihu's anger) show this mechanism operating in both moral failure and righteous indignation contexts.

Group 858-010 has a SIGNIFICANT anchor verse mismatch confirmed on per-verse assessment. AV-1 (Gen 1:31 — God saw creation as very good) does not ground the description's claims about divine seeing of affliction/pride producing compassion/judgement. AV-2 (Gen 9:22 — Ham seeing Noah's nakedness) is a morally charged human seeing, not divine. The description's content appears entirely drawn from other verses in the group not represented in the anchor set. The current dimension assignment (Theological/Divine-Human, dominant subject NONE) has low confidence. Session B must: (a) review the full verse list for this group to verify whether the description is valid for the wider group; (b) assess whether Gen 1:31 and Gen 9:22 are correctly classified in this group or should be reclassified; (c) consider whether the anchor verse should be re-designated to a verse that actually grounds the description.

The faith registry contains two analytically distinct inner-being poles: faith/faithfulness as inner orientation toward God (trust, reliance, integrity), and unfaithfulness/treachery as the inner act of betrayal (maal, bagad, bogedet). Session B should treat these as mirror-image inner-being categories — what it means for the inner person to be faithful vs faithless.

G4102G pistis (faith) [860-001] and G4100 pisteuō (believing) [866-001] represent the noun form (faith as disposition) and verb form (believing as act) of the same inner-being phenomenon. Session B must analyse these together to characterise how Scripture frames trust-reliance as both a stable disposition and an active inner act.

Three groups (872-001, 872-002, 872-007) in the faithfulness registry describe divine action — covenantal giving, compassionate giving, and eschatological transformation. The faithfulness registry operates significantly in the Theological/Divine-Human register, not primarily as human character description. Session B should map this divine-human asymmetry and assess whether 'faithfulness' as a registry label adequately captures the term natan's actual inner-being range.

fobeō (G5399) shows a tripartite inner-being structure: (1) theophanic/miraculous fear leading to worship (Spiritual/God-ward), (2) reverential life-shaping fear of God (Spiritual/God-ward), (3) practical self-interested fear before humans or consequences (Affective/Emotional). This tripartite structure is the most analytically important pattern in Registry 61 and should be the organising frame for Session B analysis.

ya.re (H3372G) groups add the divine 'Fear not' command (298-001) as a structuring element — showing the divine address to inner fear as a distinct inner-being category. The reassurance pattern (divine declaration of presence + commanded displacement of fear) is significant for understanding how the inner-being state of fear is meant to be transformed.

Luk 7:47 (anchor for group 5377-001) establishes an explicit inner-being grammar of forgiveness: the quantity of forgiveness received determines the intensity of the inner orientation of love produced — "she loved much because she was forgiven much; he who is forgiven little loves little." This is not merely a motivational claim but an inner-being causal chain: divine forgiveness (vertical release) → inner love-orientation (relational disposition). Session B for Reg 64 should examine whether this causal chain — forgiveness received → love orientation produced — is a recurring inner-being pattern across the forgiveness corpus, and how it relates to the grace-supplication chain already identified in Reg 68 (grace given → supplication produced, from DIM-068 Session C data). The hypothesis: forgiveness and grace both operate as inner-being generators that produce specific downstream inner orientations (love and supplication respectively).

agathos (G0018) groups in Reg 65 frame the inner being's relationship to goodness in four registers: (1) goodness as character source (good heart — Character/Disposition, 881-002), (2) goodness as moral command (do good — Moral/Conscience, 881-003), (3) goodness as inner conflict (want but cannot — Volitional/Will, 881-005), (4) goodness as divine attribute (only God is good — Theological/Divine-Human, 881-001). Group 881-005 is analytically most significant — the gap between inner desire for good and capacity to perform it is the heart of Pauline anthropology. Session B should examine how these four registers of goodness interact and what they reveal about the inner being's relationship to moral reality.

a.nah (H6031B) groups show affliction operating as an inner-being shaper in three modes: (1) affliction by others (1923-001 — experienced suffering), (2) affliction by God (1923-002 — purposive divine formation), (3) voluntary self-affliction (1923-003 — chosen submission). The same term covers all three, suggesting that the gentleness/meekness registry understands affliction as the formative mechanism of inner character. Session B should examine how gentleness is structurally connected to the affliction vocabulary — whether biblical gentleness is primarily the fruit of endured humbling.

The tov word family spans Theological/Divine-Human (divine goodness), Moral/Conscience (human moral character), Spiritual/God-ward (experiential good), and Character/Disposition (Spirit-fruit) — a very wide dimensional range for one term. Session B should analyse whether these are genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena or expressions of a unified category of 'goodness' that the English vocabulary separates but Hebrew integrates.

Group 890-001 (ta.cha.nun, supplication) — Dan 9:3 anchor shows earnest supplication enacted somatically: fasting, sackcloth, ashes. The body enacts what the inner person is doing — the whole person brought into alignment with the inner posture of plea. Session B should examine the somatic signature of grace-supplication: whether the body-posture vocabulary of petition (prostration, fasting, weeping, raised hands) encodes inner-being content about the Dependence/Creatureliness orientation, and how this connects to the grace registry's inner-being picture. Zec 12:10 (shared anchor with 889-001) shows that the capacity for earnest supplication is itself grace-given — the Spirit of grace produces the plea. This is the inner-being grammar of grace-response: grace first, supplication as grace-enabled response.

All three gratitude terms converge on Character/Disposition — gratitude is not a transient affective response but a stable inner reorientation of the whole person. The absence of gratitude as a diagnostic marker of inner corruption (5480-001) is a particularly significant finding. Session B should examine gratitude as a diagnostic indicator of inner-being health.

G0726 harpazō appears in two groups with contrasting inner-being engagements: [5493-001] predatory violation of inner content (Spiritual/God-ward) and [5493-002] divine protective snatching (Theological/Divine-Human). Session B should examine whether these two uses require distinct analytical treatment or represent a unified category of inner-being vulnerability and security.

Group 899-001 (G4726 stenagmos, the Spirit's inarticulate groaning in Romans 8): assigned Spiritual/God-ward. This is a pneumatological dimension unique in the C05 cluster — the Spirit interceding within the inner being through groaning that is beyond the person's own capacity for articulation. Session B should examine this group as a distinctive case of the inner being as the locus of the Spirit's intercessory activity.

The guilt registry (Reg 73) shows Spiritual/God-ward as the dominant dimension — sin and guilt are primarily framed in Scripture as relational conditions before God rather than psychological states. Session B analysis should proceed from this orientation: guilt is fundamentally a God-ward condition, with Moral/Conscience and Affective/Emotional dimensions secondary.

G4893 suneidesis in Reg 73 shows a four-dimensional conscience profile: (1) moral witness [53-001], (2) vulnerability [53-002], (3) interior foundation [53-003], (4) object of purification [53-004]. Session B should analyse these four functions as a coherent inner-being anatomy of conscience.

Group 911-002 (tsedeqah) is the only group in C10 explicitly engaging imputed righteousness — moral standing credited to the person by God. Session B should examine the theological significance of credited moral standing as a distinct inner-being category, and how it relates to the possessed moral character described in adjacent groups.

Hope in Registry 78 divides into three distinct modes: (a) hope as orientation or act directed toward God (Spiritual/God-ward — 30 groups across cluster); (b) hope as a constitutive inner quality or capacity of the person (Character/Disposition — groups 385-001, 401-002, 402-001, 968-001); (c) hope's absence, collapse, or disappointment (Affective/Emotional). Session B should assess whether these are three aspects of one dimension or three distinct inner-being engagements.

Registry 80 (humility) shows the biblical vocabulary of humility operating primarily as Character/Disposition (8 groups) with significant Theological/Divine-Human presence (4 groups). Humility is understood primarily as a character quality while simultaneously being the object of divine regard and the fruit of divine action (God humbling the proud). Group 57-002 (false humility) is analytically significant — the outward performance of abasement without genuine inner transformation. Session B should examine whether biblical humility is something the person cultivates, something God works in the person, or whether these are two aspects of the same inner reality, and how false humility is identified.

Group 5574-001 (akathartos — unclean spirit) presents the unclean spirit as an inner occupant whose presence produces inner captivity and whose expulsion by Christ restores inner freedom. Session B should examine this as a model of inner-being occupation and liberation — the inner person as the site of spiritual occupancy — and how this relates to the programme's understanding of the inner being's relationship to spiritual forces.

Groups 926-001 and 926-002 (ani H6041 — afflicted person) describe the inner experience of the victim of injustice — material need, oppression, and dependence on God for vindication. This is a perspective distinct from the perpetrator-focused groups elsewhere in C11. Session B should examine how the inner experience of being wronged relates to the cluster's primary focus on moral failure as an inner condition.

The functional presentational particles (hen H2005, hinneh H2009, hennah H2008) classified into the innocence registry produce a very wide group range — covering confession, theophany, moral verdict, wisdom reflection, and emotional experience. The innocence registry functions as a container for inner-being disclosure framing. Session B should assess whether this breadth is analytically productive or whether the verse context classifications for these particles should be re-examined.

Group 935-001 (aftharsia — incorruptibility) introduces the eschatological transformation of the person at resurrection — a dimension not well captured by existing vocabulary labels. Assigned Theological/Divine-Human as best available fit. Session B should address whether incorruptibility belongs in the integrity domain or warrants separate treatment, and how eschatological constitution of the person relates to present moral character.

[5696-001] names the Spirit's intercession within the person in wordless groanings — the intersection of the Spirit's inner work and the human inner-being in weakness. Session B should examine this group in relation to the prayer vocabulary in Reg 122, as the Spirit's intercession frames what human prayer is when language fails.

After dimension correction, 27 of 40 joy groups align with Spiritual/God-ward. Joy in Scripture is not primarily a free-floating affective state but is characteristically grounded in and directed toward God, his salvation, his presence, and his word. Session B should foreground this as the dominant finding of the registry.

Three groups name joy that persists through or is intensified by suffering: 364-001 (salad — defiant exultation), 381-002 (chairo — paradoxical joy in tribulation), and 3859-001 in Registry 42 (hēdista — most gladly in weakness). This convergent cross-term pattern names a distinct inner-being mode. Session B should examine joy-through-suffering as a structural feature rather than an anomaly.

Group 378-001 (chara — abiding inner state) is assigned Character/Disposition rather than Affective/Emotional because it names a stable characterising inner quality. This parallels gratitude in Registry 69. Both registries produce at least one group where the inner state is stable and dispositional rather than episodic. Session B should examine whether there is a category of joy-as-character-quality distinct from joy-as-affective-experience.

H4941G mish.pat (Reg 98) and H4941H mish.pat (Reg 24) are the same Hebrew term across two registries: five groups in Reg 98 and three groups in Reg 24 constitute the full inner-being profile. Session B must read both registries' mish.pat groups together for a complete analysis.

The dikaios/dikaiosunē/dikaiōsis/dikaioō family (G1342, G1343, G1344, G1347) shows a consistent tension: righteousness as divine act/attribute (Theological/Divine-Human) vs righteousness as human inner quality (Moral/Conscience). This is one of the most analytically significant tensions in the programme. Session B must examine this carefully.

The love registry spans the broadest relational vocabulary in the programme — agapē, phileō, chesed, and related terms — encompassing God's love for humanity (GOD dominant), human love toward God, mutual love among persons, and disordered love. Session B should map the full spectrum of love's inner-being architecture: what distinguishes the different forms, how they interrelate, and what the coexistence of eros/philia/agapē in the same registry reveals about the inner-being's capacity for love.

Group 535-001 (ye.di.dut — beloved of Gods soul, Reg 103) is anchored by Jer 12:7: I have given the beloved of my soul into the hands of her enemies. The phrase ye.di.dut nap.shi (beloved of my soul/nephesh) attributes an intimate inner-being category to God — God has a nephesh, and the beloved relationship is located there. This raises a significant inner-being question for Session B of Reg 103: does the love vocabulary of Reg 103 contain a sub-pattern of terms that attribute inner-being states to God with the same vocabulary used for human inner-being states? The terms ye.di.dut (beloved of my soul), ra.cha.mim (my heart yearns — Jer 31:20 in group 551-001), and the dod vocabulary (God as lover/beloved in Song and prophets) may all encode a divine inner-being reality using the same vocabulary as the human inner person. Session B should investigate whether Reg 103 contains a theological claim about divine inner-being experience, and how this connects to the programmes governing question about the inner characteristics of the human person.

Higgayon (H1902H) operates as a directionally-determined cognitive act — the same reflective faculty deployed in devout meditation toward God (975-001) and in hostile scheming against others (5883-001). The act is morally neutral in itself; direction determines its character. This is Sub-pattern A of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern identified across C02.

The atonement/propitiation vocabulary (kipper, kaphar, kapporet, hilasmos, hilastērios) within the mercy registry produces a cluster of Transformation/GOD groups where moral standing is transformed through atoning acts. Session B should examine the relationship between mercy as divine inner disposition (Relational Disposition/GOD) and atonement as its structural mechanism — how God's inner relational orientation of mercy is expressed in and through transformative acts that alter the person's standing.

Memory in biblical vocabulary is an active, covenantal, morally significant act — not passive cognitive retrieval. God's remembering produces redemptive action; Israel's commanded remembering constitutes covenant identity; forgetting is moral defection.

The mind is a moral organ before it is an intellectual one. It can be debased, blinded, corrupted, renewed, and transferred (mind of Christ). Romans 12:2 presents renewal of the nous as the locus of personal transformation.

The ye.tser (formed inclination) is the OT anthropology's closest approach to a foundational moral disposition. Gen 6:5 and 8:21 establish it as bent toward evil from youth — the dispositional substrate generating particular thoughts and plans.

The cha.shav root documents that the same mental faculty produces artistic design, moral reasoning, and malicious plotting — there is no separate creative vs rational faculty in Hebrew anthropology. God's planning uses the same root as wicked scheming.

Fronēma (the set mindset) in Romans 8:5-7 is the determinative factor for life or death, peace or enmity with God — making the mind's fundamental orientation soteriologically significant, not merely morally relevant.

Suneidēsis (conscience) is the NT's distinctive contribution with no direct Hebrew equivalent. The conscience can be weak, clear, seared, or purified (Heb 9:14), and functions as a bridge between inner life and relational/covenantal domains.

Deu 4:9 functionally links the sha.mar attentiveness cluster with the za.khar memory cluster: vigilant attentiveness is the means by which memory is maintained; forgetting is the product of failed attentiveness.

The 1 Cor 14:14-15 nous/pneuma distinction is the sharpest NT terminological boundary between mind-level and spirit-level inner operation — directly relevant to the Framework B spirit-soul-body boundary investigation.

Double-mindedness (dipsuchos/se.eph) documents inner division as a cross-testament pathology. James presents inner unity as a condition for receiving from God and for effective action; Ps 119:113 makes it a moral stance.

The Hebrews new covenant quotation (Heb 8:10; 10:16) names both dianoia (mind) and kardia (heart) as sites of internalized law — anticipating the structural relationship between Registries 112 and 183 for Session D investigation.

sha.mar family (H8104) spans four dimensions across Registry 112: Volitional/Will (G, covenantal obedience), Theological/Divine-Human (H, God's guarding), Moral/Conscience (H subset self-guarding; I attentive observation), Character/Disposition (J, vigilance). Session B: map the full sha.mar semantic range across all inner-being functions.

se.eph (4198-001, H5588) and dipsuchos (1398-001, G1374) form an OT/NT pair documenting inner division as a cross-testament moral pathology. Both assigned Moral/Conscience. Session B: analyse together as witnesses to the same inner-being phenomenon — the split allegiance that makes the person unstable before God.

ye.tser (936-001/936-002, H3336) spans Moral/Conscience (formed moral inclination toward evil or good) and Theological/Divine-Human (creaturely formation known and addressed by God). The OT's closest approach to a foundational moral disposition is simultaneously a moral reality and a creaturely-relational one. Session B: the ye.tser as the dispositional substrate of moral life.

Reg 114 contains the counter-term to disobedience: [1020-002] names Christ's obedience as the inner act that reverses Adam's. Session B should examine the obedience/disobedience polarity across Reg 114 and Reg 50 (C13) as a structurally significant inner-being opposition.

Registry 115 (passion) contributes zero desiderative groups in Phase C. Group 1023-001 names defiling passion as moral drive; 5948-001 names moral defilement; 1432-001 (deferred) names patient attentiveness. Session B should clarify what the passion registry actually covers in its term set and whether the English label accurately reflects the Hebrew/Greek semantic range.

Registry 116 (patience) shows a structural split: patience as human character quality (Character/Disposition, 7 groups) and patience as divine attribute (Theological/Divine-Human, 3 groups — 32-002, 33-002, 83-001). The divine patience groups show God's own inner disposition of forbearance modelled as the archetype of what believers are commanded to embody. Session B should examine whether biblical patience is explicitly presented as imitation of the divine disposition, and whether the vocabulary signals this connection.

Peace (#117) carries 15 terms with status=extracted_theological_anchor (818 active verses). These are divine names, titles, and eternal/hosts vocabulary — YHWH-Shalom, LORD of Hosts (H6635A/B, 292 verses), El Shaddai (H7706, 48 verses), Adonai (H0136, 187 verses), El Olam (H5769G/H, 273 verses), El Elyon (H5943/H5946, 14 verses), YHWH-Nissi, YHWH-Tsidkenu, YHWH-Tsidkenu (H6664H, 2 verses), sabaoth (G4519, 2 verses). Deliberately excluded from VCB — no verse_context records exist for these terms. Their active verses remain unflagged by design. Session B DataPrep must include these terms as theological frame context for the peace word analysis. Verse records are accessible via wa_verse_records joined through wa_term_inventory. See word_registry.notes for full term list.

The peace registry's inner-being content is unusually wide — spanning the felt inner state of rest/peace (Emotion — Positive), the inner posture of quietness before God (Moral Character/Relational Disposition), the divine giving of rest (Transformation/GOD), and God's own inner states of rest, restraint, and jealousy. Session B should examine what shalom names as an inner-being reality: is it primarily a felt state, a relational condition, a divine gift, or an eschatological reality — and how do these dimensions interrelate?

The ha.phakh word family (5949 series, 4 groups) encompasses both corrupting perversion and renewing transformation — overturn in either direction. Group 5949-001 assigns Spiritual/God-ward for the mixed group; 5949-002 assigns Affective/Emotional for affective transformation; 5949-003 confirms Theological/Divine-Human for divine inner change. Session B should examine whether perversion and transformation are genuinely polar uses of the same root, and what this reveals about the inner-being concept of transformation in Scripture.

The doxa/glory sub-cluster ([1047-001] through [1047-004]) in Reg 121 shows glory operating as: (1) supreme inner orientation target, (2) transforming agent through beholding, (3) doxological purpose, (4) eschatological hope. Session B should analyse this fourfold glory pattern as a significant inner-being framework within the praise domain.

The prayer registry shows a nearly uniform Spiritual/God-ward dimension profile. Session B should examine what distinguishes prayer from other Spiritual/God-ward orientations (praise, worship, seeking, faith) — what is specifically constitutive of prayer as an inner act: the bringing of the will and need before God.

The halakh (to go/walk) groups ([1235-001] through [1235-006]) form a coherent inner-being sub-cluster: inner movement as metaphor for inner orientation. Session B should analyse this motif — the direction of inner movement as the register of spiritual orientation (toward God, toward idols, toward death).

Wonder vocabulary divides between the divine side (Theological/Divine-Human) and the human response (Affective/Emotional). Session B should examine wonder as the inner state at the interface between human capacity and divine transcendence — the point where the inner person's capacity to comprehend is exceeded by what God is and does.

The paradidōmi (G3860) cluster in Reg 180 ([6835-001] through [6835-012]) spans 12 distinct inner-being uses: betrayal, Christ's passion, voluntary self-giving, judicial delivering over, apostolic transmission, discipleship under persecution, stewardship, continual self-entrustment, self-abandonment to desire, divine delegation, discipline for restoration, obedience to tradition. Session B must treat these 12 groups as a structured taxonomy of 'giving over/handing on' as an inner-being category.

The dōron/didōmi gift-vocabulary sub-cluster in Reg 180 shows a consistent pattern: giving as the domain where inner character is most directly revealed — generosity, greed, love, indifference, dependence, and gratitude all surface through the giving action. Session B should examine giving as a primary inner-being diagnostic.

Heb 4:12 — the Word of God divides soul and spirit as a sword divides joints and marrow. The soul cannot reliably distinguish itself from the spirit by introspection alone. The soul-spirit distinction is real but requires divine speech to make it visible — it is not self-evident to the person.

The deadness vocabulary (nekros, thanatos, ma.vet, mut) generates a consistent dual-register pattern: death as condition (Vitality/Existence) and death as moral/relational reality (Moral Character, Transformation, Relational Disposition). The same root terms name both the physical/existential fact of death and the spiritual/moral state it represents. Session B should map this dual-register explicitly — particularly the distinction between dead as condition you are in (nekros 1378-001, 1378-005; thanatos 7427-001) versus dead as act you perform or participate in (nekros 1378-002; nekroō 7422-001; thanatos 7427-003).
